data.title: Trump's Strike Freeze Lifts Markets, But The Calm Looks Fragile
data.description:
President Trump’s announcement of a halt in the strikes on Iranian infrastructure sparked a rise in risk assets on Monday (Mar. 23). It’s unclear if this is a temporary lull or a diplomatic opening that leads to a ceasefire, but risk assets found some breathing room yesterday. Commodities are still the strongest year-to-date performer for the major asset classes, but 2026 results are a bit less lopsided through yesterday’s close, based on a set of ETFs.

Raw materials continue to dominate this year, but the outperformance has become less extreme in recent days. WisdomTree Commodity ETF (GCC) is up 9.6% in 2026 – a strong run, although down sharply from the near-16% peak year-to-date performance set earlier this month.

After commodities, the best performer: foreign stocks in developed markets ex-US (VEA), posting a 2.1% gain, followed by US real estate investment trusts (VNQ), which are up 1.1%. US stocks (VTI), by contrast, are down 3.3% — the deepest year-to-date loss for the major asset classes so far this year.

The path ahead is still fraught due to a truckload of uncertainty. The main item on the agenda: Will a diplomatic solution emerge to end the war in Iran? There’s a glimmer of optimism following Trump’s announcement, but the fighting continues, and Iran has denied that any substantiative negotiations are happening. Fake or not, the news triggered a hefty decline in oil prices on Monday. The US benchmark fell below $90 a barrel, the lowest in nearly two weeks.

Even if the war ended today, energy infrastructure in the Persian Gulf region must be repaired to boost oil and natural gas exports from the Middle East to ease the supply crunch. “It will take some time to come back to the normal days we had before the war was started,” said Fatih Birol, the executive director of the International Energy Agency.

Meanwhile, markets are on the lookout for signs that the energy shock unleashed by the war will lift inflation for an extended period. Although formal inflation data arrives with a lag, several real-time benchmarks are hinting at elevated pricing pressure. The Economist reports:

Alternative Macro Signals, a consultancy, analyses millions of news articles. Their global inflation index, which has proved to be a useful predictor of official numbers, has recently risen sharply. If historical patterns hold, by July monthly global inflation could be above 0.6%. That is more than 7% on an annualized basis.

Alternative Macro Signals is not the only worrying datapoint. Truflation, a consultancy, analyses prices in real time from a wide variety of sources. Its figures suggest that this month American year-on-year goods inflation has jumped from less than 1% to nearly 3.5%. This was almost entirely the result of rising petrol prices.

Hotter inflation is a near-term risk, but the jump is still expected to be temporary, based on the Treasury market’s implied forecast via the yield spread for the nominal 5-year yield less its inflation-indexed counterpart. The current estimate is 2.53%, which is modestly below the peaks over the past year.

Despite the relative calm for the Treasury market’s outlook, it’s premature to dismiss the potential for an inflation shock of some duration. Despite Trump’s announcement, the war continues, which means that further upside inflation pressure may be brewing — a risk that markets have yet to fully price in.

“What makes this a fraught but intense moment is nobody can tell us what is going to happen on the ground in the conflict in the Middle East, and how long that lasts,” Chicago Federal Reserve President Austan Goolsbee said on Monday.

Until there’s a strong case for arguing otherwise, the near-term outlook for risk assets still looks wobbly.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: GDMN: The Reason Why Gold And Leverage Don't Mix
data.description:
Gold volatility is at a record high after the precious metal had a record-breaking year in 2025 and has now quickly retracted from its all-time highs of above $5,000 an ounce to levels of below $4,500.

Such returns are quite unusual and had happened only a handful of times throughout history and the skyrocketing volatility is confirming my thesis that we are entering into speculative territory.

During such extreme movements within the market, investors who do not make volatility bets should reduce risk. This holds true when it comes to owning gold or gold-related securities as well and in that regard owning instruments like WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Gold Miners Strategy Fund ETF (GDMN) becomes counterintuitive.

The reason why I say that is because adding gold exposure to an equity portfolio is done with the intention to reduce risk and not to maximize returns. The GDMN, however, is inherently riskier to other gold-related instruments as it combines exposure to gold mining stocks with leveraged exposure to gold through futures contracts.

This is a brief summary of the analysis I did a couple of months ago when the optimism surrounding GDMN was hovering at record high.

Nonetheless, whatever gold-related instrument one uses, it should ultimately be used as a vehicle to reduce portfolio volatility and not the other way around, unless one is willing to speculate on short-term movements of gold mining stocks.

Source: Seeking Alpha

As we all know, in this brief time-span since late December of last year we saw the price of gold spike and is now consolidating at levels of around $4,500 an ounce. During that period, however, GDMN holders experienced a nearly 15% drop, which notably larger to the one of the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX).

More importantly, the graph above clearly illustrates why instruments which track the performance of the precious metal are superior to the ones tracking that of gold mining stocks or incorporate leverage.

Even though the long-term narrative for gold remains intact, in the coming months we could see more volatility and even lower prices of the precious metal. Such a scenario does not bode well for GDMN and in my view continues to favour instruments like the Sprott Physical Gold Trust USD (PHYS) or the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD).

An Inferior Choice

The case for holding gold has not gone away simply due to the higher volatility. While we could certainly see lower prices in the near-term, the risk for the current monetary regime is still with us and with yields now heading higher (respectively bond prices expected to fall) gold remains an excellent addition to an all-equity portfolio.

The GDMN, however, could suffer tremendously in an event of a more sustained downturn of the price of gold.

The ETF has large exposure to gold mining stocks, which are highly cyclical and could suffer from a double whammy impact of falling gold prices and a potential sell-off in equities. The largest holdings of the GDMN have all experienced significant multiple contractions in the recent months and this could continue through the rest of 2026.

On top of all that, gold miners are also exposed to the risk of higher energy prices and with the skyrocketing oil & gas prices we could witness an almost perfect storm for gold equities.

As a matter of fact, the GDMN now serves a purpose of exacerbating market-wide movements when held as a part of an equity portfolio. The ETF has a beta of just slightly above 1 which translates into market risk that is at-par with the broader equity market. For comparison, the GDX - one of the major gold mining ETFs, had a consistently low beta in recent years and its beta is now standing at -1.

What's Next For Gold

The key developments from recent weeks is the shift in investors' anticipation from falling interest rates to potentially higher yields across the yield curve in the coming months.

More specifically, the short-term yields are breaking away as the recent Federal Reserve meeting has shown an increased probability of higher rates in the near future. As a result, the market's implied probabilities are pointing towards the federal funds rate target range to stay at 3.5% to 3.75% through the rest of 2026 and even by September of next year.

For comparison, the same table looked materially different just a couple of months ago in October of 2025 when I reiterated my warning to gold investors that we are now in a speculative territory.

What that means is that the front-end of the curve is now moving higher. Thus the probability of yields on the 2-year Treasury approaching their recent highs from 2022-23 period is quite high.

The myth that higher real interest rates are a problem for gold has been debunked already as we have seen the correlation between the two completely disappear in recent years. What's pointing to a potential short-term pressure for the price of gold, however, is the link between copper to gold ratio and the front-end of the yield curve.

The two variables tend to move together and for a very solid reason - when the economy is booming the price of copper goes up as gold's attractiveness as a safe-haven asset fades. This results in a higher copper-to-gold ratio at a time when the monetary policy is usually aimed at reducing money supply and driving rates higher.

The recent move in the price of gold, however, has resulted in record-low copper-to-gold ratio at a time when the yields on the 2-year Treasury have settled at relatively high levels and we saw above are likely move up in the coming months.

This sends a strong signal that the short-term price of gold has become unsustainably high and we could see more selling pressure in the coming weeks or even months. If this happens, the leveraged exposure to gold that the GDMN offers could be catastrophic for returns.

Investor Takeaway

The way that GDMN is structured offers little to no utility for most retail investors. The ETF is already significantly underperforming both the GLD and the GDX and the probability of this trend continuing over the coming months is now quite high. At times of extreme volatility investors are better-off to stick with lower risk products and such that offer a more direct exposure to the price of gold as opposed to a leveraged one.

data.title: Marsh & McLennan Remains An Attractive Growth Play In The Insurance Sector
data.description:
As I’ve covered in a previous article some months ago, I see Marsh & McLennan Companies, Inc. (MRSH) as an interesting growth play in the insurance sector due to its strong fundamentals and growth strategy based on organic efforts and acquisitions. Despite that, MRSH shares have delivered a negative return in recent months and slightly underperformed the market, due to concerns about AI and some cyclical issues.

From an operating perspective, Marsh has delivered a good performance during 2025, supported both by organic growth and the consolidation of McGriff, that was bought back in November 2024 and has now contributed to Marsh’s revenues and earnings for a full year.

In 2025, Marsh’s revenues amounted to nearly $27 billion, up by 10% on a reported basis and increasing by 4% from the previous year on an underlying basis. By operating segment, the acquisition of McGriff impacted mainly its Marsh Risk unit, which reported revenue growth of 12% YoY, or 4% on an underlying basis. This acquisition increased the weight of Risk & Insurance Services within the group, to about 64% of overall revenues in 2025.

This acquisition was also positive for the company’s operating leverage, as operating expenses increased by 11.3% to $20.7 billion during the last year. This was a slightly lower growth rate than compared to revenues, leading to some margin expansion last year. Indeed, its adjusted operating margin increased to 27.1% in 2025, representing its 18th consecutive year of margin expansion.

However, due to higher indebtedness following the acquisition of McGriff, which resulted in higher interest costs, its net income was up by only 2.5% YoY to $4.16 billion.

From a balance sheet perspective, the company’s net debt increased significantly due to McGriff’s acquisition, which was largely financed through debt. Marsh’s net debt amounted to some $19.8 billion at the end of 2025, representing a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of about 2.6x. This leverage ratio is still acceptable, but it’s considerably higher than 1.9x before the McGriff acquisition, and assuming Marsh wants to maintain an investment-grade credit rating in the near future, the company needs to reduce debt levels ahead.

This means a good part of its free cash flow generation should be used to reduce debt, even though Marsh seems to generate enough cash to maintain an attractive shareholder remuneration policy, investing for growth, and reduce debt at the same time.

Its free cash flow amounted to some $5 billion in 2025, increasing by 17% YoY and reaching about 19% of its revenues, the highest level over the past fifteen years. This bodes well for Marsh to maintain a balanced use of cash to pursue M&A, capital returns, and have a solid financial position over the medium to long term, even though I’m not expecting relatively large acquisitions in the near term as the leverage position is somewhat stretched.

Despite that, Marsh has maintained its quarterly dividend at $0.90 per share recently, which leads to a dividend yield around 2% at its current share price. This is slightly higher than when I last analyzed it due to a lower share price (it was yielding about 1.9% at the time), but it’s still not much attractive compared to other alternatives in the insurance sector.

Thus, Marsh’s income appeal is not particularly attractive to income-oriented investors, with further capital returns being made through share repurchases. In 2025, March repurchased some $2 billion of its own stock, which was more than $1.7 billion returned through dividends, a trend that is likely to be maintained ahead.

Regarding acquisitions, the company has maintained its appetite for small bolt-on acquisitions, having reached an agreement to buy an insurance agency focused on the maritime industry back in February, while more recently also reached a deal to acquire AltamarCAM. This is a specialist private markets asset manager based in Spain, which has some $20 billion of assets under management (AuM), and will be part of the Mercer unit when the deal closes during the second half of 2026.

This acquisition increases Mercer’s private markets platform, which is a significant part of its close to $700 billion of AuMs. AltamerCAM was founded more than 22 years ago and has deep expertise in this area, having some 280 employees, and has clients across the insurance industry, family offices, pension funds, banks, beyond others. The financial terms of this acquisition were not disclosed, but from a strategic point of view this is an acquisition that should add capabilities in alternative investments, being good for the long-term growth prospects of the Mercer unit.

On a more cyclical note, over the past few quarters the pricing in the Property & Casualty sector has been relatively weak following a ‘hard’ market cycle during 2023-24, which has pressured Marsh’s revenue growth to some extent. This is something that is not specific to Marsh, also affecting other players in the brokerage insurance sector, as I’ve recently covered on Arthur J. Gallagher (AJG).

This relatively weak pricing environment with declining rates across several insurance lines also continued related to reinsurance pricing in January, which is expected to put some pressure on revenue growth in its Risk & Insurance segment over the coming quarters. Despite that, Marsh’s guidance is to report underlying revenue growth during 2026 similar to last year, while targeting margin expansion due to its internal efforts to cut costs and operate more efficiently.

The market seems to agree with this view and is expecting revenue growth of about 5% annually over the next three years, which should be supported mainly by organic growth, as large acquisitions aren’t much likely due to the company’s somewhat high financial leverage. I think there is some risk for revenues to come softer than expected as pricing in the insurance and reinsurance markets are not likely to improve much in the short term, and recent revisions have been negative showing that analysts are also becoming more negative about Marsh’s top-line momentum ahead.

On top of some cyclical headwinds for revenue growth, another issue that has affected recently the insurance brokerage industry has been concerns about the potential disruption impact of AI on the business model of established players.

These concerns have increased over the past few months as agentic AI has improved and more routine tasks can now easily be replaced by AI workflows, with the market seeing insurance brokerage as an area that can potentially be disrupted by AI in the near future.

While AI can be a threat if customers start to use AI chatbots to search for better prices and offers across the insurance industry, on the other hand it can also be a tool to achieve better efficiency by automating tasks and reduce the need for headcount, potentially leading to higher business margins ahead.

As Marsh has a business that is heavily reliant on its staff, I think AI can be a double-edged sword, with some part of its business that is more focused on advising being susceptible to disruption, but on the other hand it can also lead to significant cost reductions over the long term.

The ultimate impact of AI is certainly uncertain right now, but the market seems to be already pricing the potential disruption risk, and this has been an important issue impacting negatively on the share price of insurance brokerage companies lately, of which Marsh was no exception.

Taking into account this background of some cyclical pressure on revenues, a leveraged financial profile not making large acquisitions likely ahead, and concerns about disruption risk from AI, Marsh’s valuation has declined over the past few months and is now trading at about 20x trailing earnings, compared to closer to 30x one year ago.

Based on forward earnings, its multiple is about 18.4x, being slightly lower than when I last analyzed Marsh. This valuation multiple is much below Marsh’s own history over the last five years (around 25x forward earnings), but compared to some of its closest peers, such as Arthur J. Gallagher, Aon Plc (AON), or Willis Towers Watson (WTW) it’s now trading at a premium as the market seems to see Marsh’s business at less risk from being disrupted from AI due to higher diversification and its leading profile among investment management compared to its peers.

Conclusion

Marsh has reported a positive operating performance in 2025, but recent pricing headwinds in the insurance and reinsurance sectors have been negative for its revenue growth, plus concerns about potential AI disruption have also impacted negatively its share price recently. Despite that, I continue to see Marsh as an interesting growth play in the insurance sector and see recent weakness as a buying opportunity for long-term investors, as the company’s growth prospects remain good and its valuation seems to be attractive.

data.title: Eurozone PMI Drops To 10-Month Low On Middle East Conflict
data.description:
By Bert Colijn, Chief Economist, Netherlands

Ahead of the Middle East conflict, optimism among European businesses was strong. Growth was maintaining a decent pace over previous quarters, and expectations of increased public investment were boosting hopes of a rebound among manufacturers.

But the war has put paid to hopes of short-term growth acceleration. Businesses were much less optimistic in March, according to the Purchasing Managers' Index, and reported significant increases in input costs and supply chain disruptions.

The bright spot was the manufacturing sector, where the output PMI remained broadly stable (51.7 compared to 51.9 in February). This still indicates decent output growth for now, but the mood among manufacturers for the months ahead has become more downbeat.

The eurozone’s vulnerabilities are once again laid bare. For energy-intensive industry, this means that a recovery will be harder to achieve, which matters significantly for overall production. And consumers are less confident with prices at the pump having jumped, which means that household consumption could be under pressure despite decent wage growth. The services PMI already reported a big decline, from 51.9 to 50.1.

For the eurozone economy, a return to strength depends very much on the length of the Middle East conflict. A fairly fast end would boost hopes of a more modest impact on consumer prices and would increase chances of a rebound for industry. But as today’s PMI illustrates, business conditions have worsened for the moment, and optimism is taking a hit.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: IREN's Power Moat: Winning The AI Race
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

IREN Limited (IREN) has been shaking the AI cloud industry.

IREN is a fast-pivoting AI compute company that started as a Bitcoin miner. Simply put, it offers the power and infrastructure that companies require to execute large-scale AI workloads with great speed and efficiency.

The company is already executing GPU deployments, which puts its transformation into full swing.

To say the least, the heavyweights are already on board with IREN.

For example, Microsoft (MSFT) signed a multi-year AI agreement, Nvidia (NVDA) is providing the GPUs, and Dell Technologies (DELL) is powering the major infrastructure.

Even CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius (NBIS) are also showcasing what can be done in neoclouds. However, IREN is taking a step further in its vertically integrated approach. It aligns power, hardware, and enterprise contracts in a meaningful way.

Moreover, the physical stack ownership enables IREN to convert the capacity into revenue more quickly than the competitors that depend on third-party hosts. Kent Draper, the Chief Commercial Officer of IREN, claimed in Q2 2026 earnings call,

“Demand is not the constraint for us.”

That is what backs my point. Execution, not interest, is the real constraint in AI infrastructure.

Furthermore, IREN’s initial performance is already showing results. The revenue of AI Cloud Services is increasing with Bitcoin mining financing the pivot.

The stock is currently trading at around $41. Within the present configuration, it can grow in the next few months, provided deployments remain on time. Things may go even faster in case AI revenue increases faster than predicted or new partnerships are formed.

There are risks, but I view IREN as a Buy with a long runway. Execution is already underway, and for those investors who can withstand a bit of volatility, there is clear upside potential.

The Moat: Power, Vertical Integration, and Speed to Compute

Speed and reliability cannot be purchased through money alone. Access to power, grid connections, land, and cooling systems tend to determine which companies can deploy at scale in a timely manner.

IREN converts that challenge into a benefit. It can respond faster than its competitors by owning its data centers, electrical infrastructure, and GPU hardware.

Besides, operating on 100% renewable energy provides the company with a sustainable advantage. Moreover, operational discipline and physical control become a moat, which is hard to replicate. Daniel Roberts, IREN’s Co-Chief Executive Officer pointed out in the Q2 FY26 call,

“If you haven't got the power and the capacity and the ability to execute, you're going to struggle to be a player,”

This focus on execution can be traced in how fast GPUs can be made online at Mackenzie and Childress.

Mackenzie is directly connected to the BC Hydro grid through an on-site substation, and Childress is connected to ERCOT. In simple words, the power infrastructure is already in place and GPUs can become operational immediately after deployment.

Furthermore, Morgan Stanley Research estimates the U.S. data center demand to amount to 74 gigawatts in 2028, and a possible shortage of 49 gigawatts. Hence, power control is not only operational but rather strategic. The majority of new players face difficulty here, and that lag becomes a pristine commercial opportunity for the fast movers.

In addition, scale coupled with assured demand from Microsoft and Nvidia's priority hardware increases the capability of IREN to outrun nearly anyone.

Currently, IREN has a fleet of a whopping 150,000 GPUs compared to 99,900 at the end of 2025, and more deployments are being planned through H2 2026.

Concurrently, the company has acquired more than 4.5 gigawatts of power, and 1.6 gigawatts are to be added in 2028 to serve its Oklahoma campus.

Partnerships are important too. The $9.7 billion Microsoft deal ensures demand, and Nvidia's preferred partner status assures top-priority hardware and business credibility.

What is remarkable is the manner in which the pieces are synchronized. The competitors can either buy the GPUs or rent the power but scaling up power, hardware and enterprise contracts at such a level is extremely hard. Speed is a commercial weapon with the control of full stack.

As AI capacity shortages are expected to continue up to 2026, IREN has an advantage in physical infrastructure. At the same time, it stands out in timing, execution and operations. Combined, the advantage becomes more sustainable and the company has a chance to become the leader in a supply-limited AI infrastructure market.

Riding the AI Tsunami: Revenue Engines and Market Opportunity

The physical assets of IREN are being transformed in a very conscious manner into a high-value business. In Q2 2026, AI Cloud Services revenue surged to $17.3 million from just $2.7 million a year ago.

Direct costs were only $2.4 million, which implies that the segment produced roughly $14.9 million gross contribution or 86% of revenue. These initial margins mean that this pivot is already yielding results and the company is capable of turning capacity into cash.

Interestingly, contracts are also contributing to de-risking growth. The five-year Microsoft deal worth $9.7 billion is offered with prepayment of 20%. With $3.6 billion in GPU funding, IREN will be able to finance nearly all of the GPU expenses in that deal.

Such a match between deployment and cash flow allows IREN to grow without tripping on funding gaps.

The company is not just limiting itself to the AI Cloud Services. Purpose-built data centers and colocation can deliver high-performance computing and other AI workloads with optionality, which will transform infrastructure into a series of revenue streams.

In the meantime, Bitcoin mining continues to generate cash flow, and it contributed $167.4 million in Q2 FY26. This mix strikes a balance between growth and stability. Also, it assists in financing the AI expansion and eliminates the volatility to provide the company with the opportunity to make strategic instead of reactive moves.

Here’s the bigger picture. Data centers in AI and crypto will push U.S. electricity consumption to record levels in 2026 and 2027. Globally, data centers are expected to require up to $6.7 trillion in capital by 2030. By ensuring the availability of GPUs early and power lock-in, IREN will be a first mover.

Operational execution becomes a genuine competitive advantage with the control of the infrastructure deployments' timing. It is simply about performing where others may fail to deliver and doing it with precision and discipline.

Capital, Capex, and Confidence

AI demand alone is not a route to success. The company raised $9.3 billion in capital in the last eight months based on customer prepayments, convertible notes, GPU leasing, and financing.

Alongside, it posted over $9.2 billion in year-to-date financing in Q2 FY26, and it had $2.8 billion in cash at the beginning of this year. These sources give a powerful financial runway to enable the gradual deployment of GPUs.

In addition, to facilitate the March 2026 GPU expansion, IREN anticipates additional CAPEX of about $3.5 billion in H2 2026, which includes GPUs, servers, storage, networking, labor, and ancillaries.

Notably, payment conditions are designed on a post shipment basis, and this takes the pressure off working capital and spending cycles are kept close to commercial landmarks. This strategy shows operational discipline and a gradual way up the ladder.

In turn, deployment is measured. The 50,000-plus Nvidia B300 GPUs will be available in phases through H2 2026 at Mackenzie and Childress.

Microsoft deployments are being done on the Childress site in a similar phased rollout with the use of liquid-cooled infrastructure that supports 200 megawatts of critical IT load. The coordination of the hardware deployment with the power and cooling readiness allows the company to minimize the risk of the operations and speed up the AI capacity. As Daniel Roberts, Co-CEO, stated in the Q2 call,

“It is all about the three Cs and bringing those together and doing it in a way where you are maximizing the opportunity for shareholders.”

Apparently, dilution is an issue at the shareholder level and this will be dealt with in the risks section of the thesis. This risk is, however, reduced by asset-backed financing and prepayments by customers.

Particularly, Microsoft's prepayment of $1.9 billion and GPU financing provide a secured cash flow. This minimizes reliance on equity markets and matches deployment with funding certainty.

Valuation Discussion

Now here’s when things get interesting. IREN’s valuation centers on growth potential and execution.

The revenues are estimated to be $1.01 billion in June 2026 and $2.91 in June 2027. The forward price-to-sales multiples are expected to dip from 13.55x to 4.72x from 2026 to 2027. What this basically means is that the market is appreciating the capability of the company to convert the GPU capacity and the guaranteed power into cash.

It is not a narrative of short-term earnings. The actual play is seizing the time-sensitive AI demand before competitors can scale.

Indeed, AI Cloud Services delivers impressive gross margins. That being said, the weighing down of IREN’s net earnings can’t be ignored. This is due to operating expenses, depreciation from recent data center buildouts and Financing costs related to GPU acquisitions.

As a result, analysts estimate a small profit in 2026 and a possible loss in 2027. Investors are not purchasing the stock at present profits, but they are acquiring the growth runway and big enterprise deals.

That is all that matters in execution. Provided that IREN is able to roll out GPUs without any issue and ensure that they are kept occupied, the revenue will flow, but a single slip may change the outcomes radically. It is a very high-potential and high-stakes opportunity.

Furthermore, IREN is trading at a distinct premium compared to sector. The trailing twelve months price-to-sales is 14x versus the sector average of 3.1x. Forward EV/EBITDA is 30.6x compared to 12.9x and the forward price-to-book ratio is 2.7x.

However, these multiples translate into a tangible benefit. What IREN is doing with power, the infrastructure, and priority hardware is not something you can easily replicate. Other competitors may purchase GPUs, but it is a different challenge to move as fast and as smoothly as IREN did at this level.

The stock trades technically at $41.29 (as of writing). In the past month, IREN has been trading between $38 and $44. Well, this shows a short-term consolidation. Additionally, the 10-day simple moving average stands at $41.49, which reflects the possibility of pressure in the short run.

In the meantime, the 50 and 100-day averages of $46.21 and $47.35 represent resistance, and the 200-day average of $37.71 represents support. As we can assess, although the momentum has slowed down, the long-term upward trend is still intact.

Using a base case in which AI Cloud Services revenue would grow to $2.5 billion by the end of 2026 and a 6-8x revenue multiple, the AI segment alone would be worth a valuation of between $15 and $20 billion.

The base case target price would be around $45 per share based on $332.28 million shares outstanding. This leaves ample room for upside in case the utilization and execution of the GPU remain on schedule.

Risks and Takeaway

For starters, execution is the biggest risk. Purchasing GPUs can be easily announced, but the actual work is placing them into the power-ready, cooled and networked data centers.

IREN intends to deploy over 50,000 Nvidia B300 GPUs in H2 2026. Any lag in grid access, liquid cooling, or networking can potentially postpone AI revenue, speed up utilization, and jeopardize business contracts on an enterprise scale.

Additionally, this risk is increased through customer concentration. The company depends significantly on its Microsoft five-year deal. Although this deal confirms that demand is real, failure to meet delivery milestones can put the contract at risk.

The competition is also fierce. Neocloud providers are entering into huge capacity contracts. The pressure of pricing and the tight schedules imply that even minor hiccups in the execution process might result in the loss of market share or profit margins.

Investors should certainly be on the lookout for funding and dilution. Although over a large portion of the costs is financed by $3.6 billion of GPU financing and customer prepayments, there is still up to $6 billion of share issuance that can be covered under the at-the-market equity program. When IREN overloads this, the existing shareholders may face dilution, which would lower the per-share value as the business increases even more.

With this said, the relieving element is that much of the expansion is already supported by prepayments and asset-backed GPU financing, which restricts the necessity of urgent equity increases. IREN is prudent in deployment and capitalizing on the reality of a project milestone, which decreases the need to issue new shares.

The current stock price reflects IREN’s growth trajectory and presents a good entry point to long-term investors. In case IREN achieves its deployment and utilization targets, the upside would be worth witnessing.

data.title: Super Micro: Here We Go Again
data.description:
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) is a stock I've covered quite a few times. The stock is down hard yet again, as if it just can't catch a break. I wanted to take another look, and while a federal case is not welcome for investors, it ultimately just comes back to the numbers for me.

Summary of Previous Thesis

When I wrote about SMCI last November, its main problem was its gross margins. Not only that, operating cash flows turned negative as they hurried to accumulate inventory to meet demand, forcing them to raise capital as well. Puzzled by the "strong demand" but worsening margins, I observed:

I think it begs the question of why strong demand does not give them more ability to price higher, and maybe it's because they are too low on the rung of AI to do that.

Indeed, they were not as dominantly positioned as, say, Nvidia (NVDA) with its GPUs or Palantir (PLTR) with its AI-driven data analytics. All they really do is mass-produce hardware that supports these things, but clearly it's a very capital-intensive business. I worried that it might be too much so. Suspecting that it could take a long time for margins to improve or for free cash flow to appear, I rated them a Hold.

Arrest of Co-Founder

SMCI crashed again late last week. This followed an announcement that three individuals, which included an SMCI co-founder, Yih-Shyan Liaw, and an employee, were arrested on charges of smuggling Nvidia chips into China.

It should be noted that Super Micro itself was not named or accused in these charges, only the individuals.

The company announced it is cooperating with authorities in the investigation. Liaw's resignation also soon followed.

After trading fairly flat for the past month, mostly around $30 per share, the stock is now down 35% for the past month. This may seem like a sharp reaction to one news story, but the market likely does not view this in isolation. In 2024, SMCI crashed after their independent auditor resigned, causing a delay in their Form 10K. That delay coincided with a short report against the company and provoked memories of similar problems in the past that led to a fine in 2020.

All of that to say, there are multiple years of problems related to reporting and transparency, and that likely explains why the market's reaction was so strong.

Recent Financials

Even so, SMCI's value is going to depend on its financial reality. These criminal charges are a headache, but more important is the financial updates since my last coverage.

To start, revenue growth has remained very strong. Even the last couple of quarters show the company reaching new heights.

Yet, while Q2's revenue was 123% from the prior year and 153% sequentially, gross margins continued to take a hit. When I wrote last time, GM was struggling to stay around 10%. Now it's as low as 6.4%, and operating cash flow, while up, is still negative.

The impact here isn't simply a smaller gross margin. Gross profit overall declined for the first six months of fiscal 2026, from $1,446M to $1,265M. Operating expenses, meanwhile, increased with the growth.

Looking at the balance sheet, we can see that they have about $21.6B in inventory and accounts receivable. Debt has remained flat, and so if they want to replace that inventory, nearly all of the cash generated from monetizing these items is now doing it at much weaker margins.

The charges brought against Liaw seem worse in some ways, but even if that weren't the case, there's this financial reality staring at us. Every time I look at SMCI, it seems like it struggles to ride the growth of AI in a beneficial way.

Outlook and Valuation

Back to $21 per share and a market cap of $12.3B, SMCI might look undervalued. If gross margins can get back to about 13%, then they would probably be doing about $1B in FCF semiannually. That would make the current price quite a low multiple.

In the earnings call, CEO Charles Liang explained how and why gross margins should improve:

And now, I mean, product is getting mature. So those expedite transportation costs will be dramatically reduced and tariff impact also improving. And -- so overall, especially DCBBS also increasing for our -- for our gross margin. So I believe our gross margin will start to improve quarter after quarter.

With $40B in revenue being their guidance for the full year, that also suggests about $23B in the next two quarters. There's a possibility here that cash flow could at least be breakeven, if not positive. Of course, they still need to contain overhead costs while they grow as well.

Liang was also saying that not long before the war in the Middle East took off, something that he wasn't expecting to happen. I wonder how much supply disruption in fuel and energy will affect margins as they are trying to stabilize things. Even slight impacts are important when gross margins are this tight.

There's too much kicking SMCI around like hackysack for me to rate this a Buy. A margin of safety is sorely needed here. I'd prefer an entry price of $15 per share or less, which at least ensures SMCI is soon a Price/FCF multiple under 10x if gross margins improve, if.

Conclusion

Super Micro's problem isn't simply controversy. It has a hard time scaling up in a cost-effective way, and it's capital-intensive on top of all of that. While these aren't necessarily pertinent to their controversies, one has to wonder if there are underlying execution problems that lead to these sort of things. Waiting for improved gross margins or a better price, I reaffirm my Hold on this stock. SMCI remains a somewhat discouraging AI investment.

data.title: Sarepta Therapeutics: A $2B Business Priced Like It's Broken
data.description:
Thesis

Strategically, I think Sarepta Therapeutics' (SRPT) story is now going to be a fine balance between execution risk and long-term optionality. Elevidys is still the key value driver; it's still supported by strong three-year clinical data showing durable functional benefits, but a number of things have hurt the revenue predictability of the therapy. Elsewhere, the expanding siRNA pipeline across multiple rare diseases could introduce some significant upside, but, as you know, this comes with high upfront costs and clinical uncertainty. So 2025 was a bit of a transition year with Sarepta moving from a profitable rare-disease commercial company to a now very capital-intensive, multi-platform biotech. The expectation from management is that today’s elevated spending should translate into future growth and diversification. However, I personally think we need to wait for some more positive signals among all the uncertainty.

FY25 results

As you know, Sarepta’s FY25 results showed us some strong top-line momentum but heavily pressured profitability. FY25 revenue jumped 16% to $2.2 billion, which was mainly driven by both their established PMO exon-skipping franchise and continued uptake of Elevidys. The latter here contributed about $900 million. However, we have to mention here that this growth masks underlying volatility in that 4Q25 revenue fell very sharply by almost 33% year-over-year to $442.9 million. Now, this was primarily because Sarepta paused Elevidys shipments to non-ambulatory patients in the U.S. What this shows us is just how dependent near-term performance is on regulatory scope and labelling decisions. A small bit of relief came from collaboration and manufacturing revenues, especially those tied to Roche, which helped offset some of that decline.

As for profitability, the numbers here deteriorated quite significantly despite higher annual revenue. The company swung to a hefty GAAP net loss of $713 million in 2025, versus the $235 million income we saw back in 2024. I think we can put this loss down to a combination of surging R&D spending, which jumped about $718 million since last year, a pretty sharp increase in cost of sales, which more than doubled due to inventory write-downs and manufacturing issues, and lastly some milestone payments tied to its RNAi pipeline, notably to Arrowhead. I understand that these are not purely operational inefficiencies, but the scale of the swing to a net loss is a bit concerning. However, they do reflect a rather deliberate strategic shift from Sarepta here, in that they’re reinvesting aggressively into next-generation therapies, especially the siRNA platform. This will inevitably come with costs, but they still need to show they can absorb the growing pains from scaling gene therapy manufacturing. I think the spike in inventory reserves and product write-offs also suggests those demand forecasting and production alignment challenges, particularly around Elevidys after the label change.

As for the balance sheet, in some way, the company is stabilising. Sarepta managed to end 2025 with about $954 million in cash. And yes, this figure is down a lot from $1.5 billion the prior year, but it’s still substantial. We also have to keep in mind that they refinanced a large portion of their 2027 convertible debt into 2030 notes, so that should reduce near-term debt pressure. So, management here is clearly prioritising financial flexibility to fund the current pipeline, even at the expense of near-term earnings as we just saw. If we look at the restructuring program, including a headcount reduction, it also lowered SG&A expenses, so there’s definitely an effort to control fixed costs while preserving that R&D intensity. Importantly here, management is guiding toward profitability and positive cash flow sometime in 2026. That can only suggest they have a lot of confidence that Elevidys utilisation will recover. This has to be particularly true for international sales. I also see the benefit of not having the one-time costs from 2025 repeat at the same magnitude.

The importance of Elevidys revenue

As you probably know, Elevidys' revenue is fundamentally pretty hard to read right now. If you think about it, the therapy is kind of at the intersection of gene therapy economics, regulatory shocks, and operational time lag. All of this isn’t helping with revenue predictability and is a large reason the stock has been on a slow downward trend in recent times. Now, unlike Sarepta’s older exon-skipping drugs, which we used to see being chronically dosed and creating a recurring revenue base, Elevidys is only a one-time treatment, albeit with a hefty price tag. So it means each quarter basically starts from zero, and revenue is then entirely dependent on how many new patients actually get infused in that period.

So the nature of the treatment definitely creates lumpiness here, but what made it worse has been the regulatory disruption in 2025. Originally, Elevidys had a broad label, so full approval in ambulatory patients and accelerated approval in non-ambulatory patients. This essentially allowed Sarepta to cover a pretty wide portion of the Duchenne population. However, after multiple cases of fatal acute liver failure in non-ambulatory patients, the FDA forced a label change. Sarepta then paused shipments, and the agency ultimately removed the non-ambulatory population entirely and added a boxed warning, so not a good thing by any means. It left the addressable population as ambulatory patients. So that materially shrinks the addressable market and, more importantly, damages physician confidence. So if a physician is explaining to a parent about instances of fatal acute liver failure, I wouldn’t be surprised if they decline the treatment, whether the patient is ambulatory or not. So that would definitely slow down new patient starts, even in the remaining eligible group.

Now, we have to add on top of that the 6-month built-in delay. I think this is pretty critical to understanding the current guidance reset. You see, from the moment a doctor decides to treat to when the patient actually receives the therapy and revenue is recognised, it usually takes 4 to 6 months due to logistics. They need genetic confirmation, insurance approval, manufacturing of a personalised dose, and then they can schedule an infusion. So today’s revenue really reflects physician decisions made almost two quarters ago, not the current demand. I think this is why Sarepta cut near-term guidance, from what was tracking closer to $500 million to something closer to a $300 million run-rate in the management discussion. So even if demand stabilises or improves today, I don’t see there being enough calendar time in 2026 for that demand to convert into revenue.

As for the overall return to profitability guidance in 2026, it will depend on at least one thing: the conversion of demand into consistent infusion volume. Management’s view here is that they can try to fix this with a commercial push. We’ve just seen that they announced doubling the sales force, increasing physician education, and are actively trying to identify untreated patients and referring them to infusion centres. But the key friction they’re addressing is not only awareness, but also decision complexity. We have to keep in mind that Elevidys is irreversible, and you generally can’t redose AAV gene therapy. So that’s why physicians are so cautious and would generally tend to weigh more on safety vs long-term efficacy. Sarepta here is leaning heavily on 3-year durability data from EMBARK to strengthen that efficacy side of that equation. Now, if that sales push does see some upside, it should increase start forms today, which will then translate into revenue about 6 months later.

As for the remaining overhangs, there’s still the non-ambulatory setback. This group was actually very clinically important because it usually represents patients with more advanced disease, and initially, we saw this as a more meaningful part of the original growth narrative. So losing it doesn’t just reduce the market; it introduces those safety limitations of the therapy platform, as I mentioned. It also means Sarepta has to spend big if they want to try and fix it. They’re now running a study, Cohort 8, to reduce liver toxicity or acute liver injury, with the hope of regaining that population. So that in itself is a risk; they have to invest in research, and one of the outcomes is the failure to regain that non-ambulatory population. However, if they do succeed, it will reopen a major growth lever for them. The problem I see here is that if they fail, the long-term ceiling for Elevidys is then structurally lower, and the drug will just remain confined to a much narrower, earlier-stage population. And that downside scenario is exactly what the market is pricing in and why the valuation is screening as so cheap.

Amondys 45 and Vyondys 53 update

As for Sarepta’s older, steady cash-generating franchise, we just recently got an update on some of their exon-skipping PMO therapies. Both Amondys 45 (casimersen) and Vyondys 53 (golodirsen) are currently approved in the U.S. under the FDA’s accelerated approval pathway. So that allows earlier market access based on surrogate endpoints. In this case, the endpoints were increased dystrophin production in muscle. However, that approval was conditional in that Sarepta had to demonstrate real clinical benefit in confirmatory trials or risk losing the approval. This is where the ESSENCE Phase 3 study comes in; it was designed to confirm whether these drugs can actually slow disease progression in DMD. But the complication here is that ESSENCE technically missed its primary endpoint, even though a post-hoc adjusted analysis, which excluded COVID-disrupted patients, came very close to significance. The long-term real-world data also suggest some meaningful benefits, like delayed loss of mobility and respiratory decline.

So as a result, what Sarepta is now attempting to do is a bit unconventional, but I think strategically it’s very critical. They’re aiming to convert these drugs from accelerated to full traditional approval using a combination of that clinical data and real-world evidence. The good news is that the FDA is signalling some openness to reviewing that package. And if it were to be successful, this would remove a pretty hefty regulatory overhang. You see, the big risk for a long time with these two therapies has been withdrawal. They would also have a more durable commercial positioning.

This is quite important since, despite all the focus on Elevidys gene therapy, their PMO drugs still make up a rather large and stable portion of revenue. But as you know, the market is usually overly sceptical about instances such as this and what makes for bad viewing here is the reliance on non-statistically significant trial data and real-world evidence. Naturally, that will introduce some uncertainty around FDA acceptance. So do see this filing as effectively a high-stakes regulatory inflexion point in that approval would definitely solidify a core revenue stream for them and reduce the downside risk, but a rejection here would reopen concerns about the long-term viability of Sarepta’s legacy franchise.

Valuation

As for a valuation, Sarepta’s multiples are looking pretty compressed compared to their own history and the peer group. I think the most relevant way to frame it, given the inconsistent earnings, is via revenue- and enterprise-value-based multiples rather than earnings for now. On that note, the company is trading at just 0.8x trailing EV to sales and 1.1x forward EV to sales, which would be a 65% to 75% discount to sector medians and an even steeper discount to its own historical range, we’ve previously seen in the 7x to 8x range. Elsewhere, the forward EV to EBITDA sits at 3.9x and EV to EBIT at 5.1x. Both are deeply discounted, which would suggest the market is pricing in either a sharp deterioration in profitability or low confidence in the durability of future earnings.

I think what the market is effectively discounting here is execution risk around Elevidys. So much of the potential upside rests on those sales, so it’s led to a lot of scepticism about the sustainability of growth and margins. The steep discount would also imply some concerns that those 2025 disruptions, like shipment pauses, safety-related label changes, inventory write-downs, and manufacturing inefficiencies, may not be temporary. What investors now want to know is whether Elevidys can reaccelerate, especially in non-ambulatory patients, and whether the expensive RNAi pipeline will actually translate into commercially viable products. What I’m trying to say here is that, despite nearly $2.2 billion in annual revenue, Sarepta is being valued more like a distressed/structurally challenged biotech than a growth platform.

Looking Ahead

Overall, I think the hold case here still remains intact. Sarepta is no longer a clean growth story but deep in a transition where execution on Elevidys and pipeline validation are going to determine the potential upside. I do think the company still has meaningful long-term optionality with their gene therapy and their expanding siRNA platform, but the near-term visibility is just so limited by the factors I mentioned.

For investors, 2026 is going ot have a few key data readouts. We have the continued follow-up from the EMBARK study supporting durability, early pipeline readouts in siRNA programs expected sometime in early 2026, and also the initial progress from the Cohort 8 study, which is aimed at addressing non-ambulatory safety.

data.title: The 2026 Market: A World Of Constant Rotation
data.description:
By Jeremy Schwartz, CFA

The market environment entering 2026 has been defined by two seemingly contradictory forces.

On one side, a handful of technology companies, especially those tied to artificial intelligence infrastructure, have dominated headlines and index weights. The “Magnificent Seven” have at times represented more than one-third of the S&P 500’s total weight1.

Yet leadership beneath the surface has rotated rapidly. AI enthusiasm has created pockets of extreme valuation, while other profitable businesses, sometimes in the very same sectors, have been sold aggressively.

In classic equity frameworks, the market is divided into growth and value. But when the same company can oscillate between “expensive growth story” and “temporary bargain,” the lines begin to blur.

That is where a fundamentally driven approach with active management sensitive to value becomes particularly important.

The WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund (WTV) focuses on companies generating strong shareholder returns through dividends and buybacks while maintaining attractive valuations.

WTV’s portfolio can sometimes look very different from traditional value indices.

A Different Definition of Value

S&P style indices classify companies using separate growth and value scores based on standardized financial factors. The value score incorporates three valuation ratios:

  • Book-to-price,
  • Earnings-to-price and
  • Sales-to-price.

Companies are ranked based on their growth and value characteristics, with roughly one-third of market capitalization allocated to value, one-third to growth and the remainder split proportionally between the two styles depending on each company’s profile. This process can lead to some surprising outcomes if looking for real value stocks.

Companies that many investors associate with higher valuations—and not traditional “value”—still appear in the S&P 500 Value Index. Under S&P’s framework, a stock doesn’t need to be “cheap” for a portion of its market cap to be allocated to Value. If a stock falls into the “blend” zone, its market cap can be split across both the Value and Growth indices. As a result, several high-profile companies receive meaningful weight in Value.

The takeaway isn’t that these stocks have become “value” in the traditional sense—many even have negative value-factor z-scores in S&P’s own scorecard—but that the methodology assigns weight across both indices when a company’s profile is not extreme enough to be classified entirely as Growth.

In short, a stock can screen expensive on traditional valuation metrics and still land partly in the Value Index if its growth and momentum characteristics aren’t strong enough to place it exclusively in Growth.

Take Tesla (TSLA) as an example. Tesla has sharply negative z-scores across the three value factors. But because its growth-factor z-scores are not comparably strong (with only 12-month momentum positive), a portion of its market cap is allocated to the Value Index.

Figure 1: Factor Z-Scores vs. S&P 500

Source: WisdomTree, FactSet, as of 11/30/25. WisdomTree replication of S&P Style Rebalance scores. Factor labels reflect the S&P factor score inputs (z-scores). B/P = Book-to-Price, E/P = Earnings-to-Price, S/P = Sales-to-Price, 3yr Sales Growth = Trailing 3 Year Sales per Share Growth, 3yr EPS Change/Price = 3 Year Change in EPS divided by last price, 12mo Mom= Trailing Twelve Month total return.

Figure 2 highlights the 10 largest S&P 500 constituents (by index weight) whose market cap is split between the Value and Growth indices. Notably, Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN) and Tesla—each with positive Style Scores (more Growth than Value)—still show up as meaningful weights in the Value Index because their market caps are so large relative to the rest of the Value sleeve.

Apple is the top holding in the S&P 500 Value Index (7.3%) and the fourth-largest holding in the S&P 500 Growth Index (6.4%). Yet on traditional valuation measures, it trades well above the broader market, which many investors would not associate with classic value.

Figure 2: Top 10 Split Constituents in the S&P 500 Style Rebalance

Sources: WisdomTree, S&P, FactSet. Data is as of the year-end S&P rebalancing announcement which took place in December 2025. Holdings subject to change.

A Potentially “Purer” Value: WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund

WTV is designed to be more intentionally value-oriented. Rather than relying on relative style scores that must balance market cap, WTV targets shareholder yield (dividends plus buybacks) and uses additional quality-oriented screens to help avoid classic value traps.

WTV is also actively managed, and its process can identify stocks that screen as high quality value.

The end result is a portfolio that is meaningfully cheaper on a forward P/E (Figure 3a) and materially higher on shareholder yield (Figure 3b) than the S&P 500 Value Index.

Following the most recent rebalance, WTV’s shareholder yield sits around 7.0%, roughly double that of the S&P 500 Value Index at roughly 3.5%.

At the same time, the fund maintains attractive valuations, with a forward price-to-earnings ratio of about 12.8x, compared with 19.1x for the S&P 500 Value Index.

Figure 3a: Forward P/E Over Time

Figure 3b: Shareholder Yield Over Time

Sources for 3a and 3b: WisdomTree, FactSet, S&P, 12/29/17-3/11/26. Period chosen to coincide with the start of the shareholder yield-focused investment process of the WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund in December 2017. You cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

WTV is not simply chasing cheap stocks. It is identifying companies that are reasonably valued and actively returning capital to shareholders.

Broad Diversification Without Megacap Concentration

WTV typically holds around 120 companies, allowing the portfolio to focus on businesses with stronger shareholder-return characteristics.

Interestingly, more software companies are increasingly showing up in the top 10 holdings due to their high levels of share buybacks that are reducing shares outstanding—a reversal of their high valuations 4-5 years ago.

The current portfolio also tends to avoid the heavy concentration that has characterized many broad market benchmarks.

For example, the Magnificent Seven collectively represent more than 34% of the S&P 500, yet WTV currently holds only one of those companies—NVIDIA (NVDA)—at a 3% weight, far below its S&P 500 Index weight2.

We see this in Figure 4.

Figure 4: March 2026 Rebalance Shifts Exposure to Certain Tech Names

Source: WisdomTree, FactSet. Rebalance effective at the close of 3/11/26. Holdings subject to change.

That positioning reflects an important principle of the strategy:

The goal is not to avoid large technology companies—but to own them only when valuations make sense. The strategy repositioned from Meta Platforms (META) towards Nvidia and Salesforce (CRM)—but also has top 10 weights in Zoom (ZM), Cisco (CSCO) and Adobe (ADBE).

What we find fascinating in the case of Nvidia, a company with a market capitalization above $4 trillion, is the forward price-to-earnings ratio is very similar to that of the overall S&P 500 Index. But, as we see in Figure 5:

  • Nvidia’s trailing 5-year earnings growth clocked in at 95.1%, annualized. The market came in at 19.0% per year.
  • On a forward basis, Nvidia’s earnings are forecast to grow at 25.7% per year, as opposed to the market at 16.2%.

Bottom line: Nvidia’s growth trajectory at a valuation similar to the market represents, to us, a significant value opportunity.

Figure 5: Nvidia’s Price Relative to its Growth Rate Looks Compelling

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, S&P. *Forward price-to-earnings and earnings growth based on median analyst estimates. **Trailing 3-year where 5-year is not available. Growth is annualized. ***Estimated Long-Term Growth is annualized and based on median analyst earnings growth estimates over the next 3 years. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bigger Picture

The investment landscape of 2026 continues to be shaped by rapid technological change, geopolitical shifts, and evolving monetary policy.

In that environment, valuation discipline may matter more than ever.

The WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund seeks to capture that discipline by focusing not simply on low multiples, but on companies generating tangible returns for shareholders while trading at attractive valuations.

Having a shareholder yield that is approximately double that of traditional S&P 500 value indices shows how differentiated the positioning is.

Sometimes those companies will look like traditional value stocks.

Other times they may include world-class technology firms temporarily caught in a market rotation.

As investors wonder about the overall valuation levels of the market by and large, we think the close to 7% shareholder yield of WTV is far from expensive—it is a truly differentiated value portfolio.

1Source: Daly, L. (2026). The Magnificent Seven’s market cap vs. the S&P 500. The Motley Fool.

2Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet. Rebalance effective at the close 2/12/2026. Holdings subject to change.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. Value stocks, as a group, may be out of favor with the market and underperform growth stocks or the overall equity market. Funds focusing their investments on certain sectors increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. While the Fund is actively managed, the Fund’s investment process is heavily dependent on quantitative models and the models may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Jeremy Schwartz, CFA, Global Chief Investment Officer

Jeremy Schwartz has served as our Global Chief Investment Officer since November 2021 and leads WisdomTree’s investment strategy team in the construction of WisdomTree’s equity Indexes, quantitative active strategies and multi-asset Model Portfolios. Jeremy joined WisdomTree in May 2005 as a Senior Analyst, adding Deputy Director of Research to his responsibilities in February 2007. He served as Director of Research from October 2008 to October 2018 and as Global Head of Research from November 2018 to November 2021. Before joining WisdomTree, he was a head research assistant for Professor Jeremy Siegel and, in 2022, became his co-author on the sixth edition of the book Stocks for the Long Run. Jeremy is also co-author of the Financial Analysts Journal paper “What Happened to the Original Stocks in the S&P 500?” He received his B.S. in economics from The Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania and hosts the Wharton Business Radio program Behind the Markets on SiriusXM 132. Jeremy is a member of the CFA Society of Philadelphia.

Original Post

data.title: Inflation-Proof Your Retirement Income With These 2 Picks
data.description:
It seems that the inflation dragon will once again return to disrupt the markets, our portfolios, and, importantly, the purchasing power of our precious savings (investments).

The war in Iran, which has, among other things, triggered soaring oil prices, will inevitably translate into higher inflation. We still don't have visibility on the level of consequences, but it is clear that we can forget about reaching a long-term 2% inflation target any time soon. The market also agrees with it by basically eliminating interest rate cut probabilities for the remainder of 2026.

The thing is that these dynamics come on top of an already relatively elevated inflation level, which for February 2026 came in at 2.4% (these data points exclude the effects from surging oil prices). Interestingly, exactly the energy component was among the few items that held back the overall CPI from rising even further.

Given the magnitude of change in oil prices (including gas, power and crude oil derivatives), it is a story of not only inflation but also recession, with some of the largest banks already recalibrating the odds for a recession to the upside.

The fact that the overall equity market (SPY) and certain above-average risk areas of the market, such as leveraged fixed income (PDO) and high-growth AI names (QQQ), have been selling off is another sign, which confirms that the problem has two sides: inflation and recession.

For retirement income investors, all this imposes yet another set of headaches.

On the one hand, it would make sense to de-risk (at least on the margin) portfolios from very high-yield names to avoid negative surprises, which might come in case the recession really materializes.

On the other hand, sacrificing yield in the moment, when the purchasing power of in-place current income streams is about to face significant headwinds from rising inflation, is not the smartest idea either (e.g., some might not afford it).

In this article I will elaborate on two picks, which:

  • Offer tangible yields from the start.
  • Carry recession-proof fundamentals.
  • Embody robust inflation pass-through mechanics.

In my view, these are the names that could be deemed as direct beneficiaries from the prevailing macro (and geopolitical) mess and thus have to be at least considered by prudent income investors when thinking about where to deploy incremental capital (or how to reshuffle a bit the existing capital).

#1: BEP, 5.1%

Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. Limited Partnership Units (BEP)(BEP.UN:CA) is a renewable energy-focused franchise. It develops, owns and operates various renewable asset technologies, which are spread across different geographies (the U.S. is a main one).

The business model is relatively simple: acquire or lease land, get permits, find long-term power offtakers (users) and then, based on that guaranteed contract, source financing and develop the asset base.

What is important to understand from the start is that BEP is not focused on offshore wind, residential solar or renewable technology development. Plus, it does not take market or the so-called merchant risk (i.e., most of the cash flows stem from long-term power purchase agreements, which are contracted with investment-grade corporates).

In the table we can see how this looks in practice:

Besides the cash flow predictability that these contracts bring to the table, they also introduce a timely inflation-indexation factor, which as of Q4 2025 was relevant for ~70% of the total revenues.

So, if inflation goes up (which is very likely), BEP should be able to benefit from that and thus grow its distributions at a faster pace than before.

On top of the embedded CPI escalators, we have to factor in the positive effects from the retained FFO reinvestments (~29% of the total proceeds) and already contracted organic growth pipeline (target is to install 10 GW per year), which will be feeding into cash flow growth going forward.

It is worth noting that BEP has structured its investment grade balance sheet in an optimal way, which allows the business to shield the cash flows from potentially rising borrowing costs, which, theoretically, could offset the benefits from CPI escalators. For example, the corporate debt has a weighted average debt maturity of 13 years, and 100% of it is fixed. The project-level debt has an average term of 10 years, and 96% of it is fixed.

In my view, BEP is nicely positioned on all fronts:

  • It is set to enjoy tailwinds from increased demand and rising prices of power (e.g., think of AI data centers, the need to have off-grid power projects, etc.).
  • The forthcoming inflation is not a risk but an opportunity to enhance the cash flows.
  • The business model is resilient, durable and underpinned by a strong and interest-rate-protected balance sheet.

#2: PAA, 7.7%

Plains All American Pipeline, L.P. Common Units (PAA) is a pure-play midstream infrastructure partnership, which is subject to K-1 forms (just as BEP). However, just as for BEP - Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC) - there is an available "non K-1" alternative, Plains GP Holdings (PAGP).

Another thing that these two machines have in common is the durable, resilient and inflation-protected business model.

About 85% of PAA's EBITDA stems from fee-based contracts, which provide long-term cash flow certainty. These contracts come with minimum volume commitments, price risk protection and annual fee escalators.

PAA is also a direct beneficiary of the rising crude oil prices. There are two avenues through which PAA can extract a value from the prevailing dynamics:

  • Each $10/bbl change in WTI price above the ~$62.5 bbl (on this figure the 2026 EBITDA guidance was built) contributes to ~1.5% in incremental EBITDA growth.
  • Each 100 Mb/d change in the Permian Basin production level warrants ~0.375% of incremental EBITDA generation. For example, if the production increases by 10% from the level that was baked into the 2026 guide, the EBITDA would advance by ~4%.

Here it is worth noting that the 2026 guidance itself (excluding the effect from the Iran war or the oil shock) indicates a year-over-year EBITDA growth of 13%.

Currently, it is too early to quantify the impact for PAA's EBITDA, but it is clear that we will most probably be talking about meaningfully stronger results than what was communicated earlier this year.

Now you might wonder what the "so what" is here.

Let's take a look at the chart below:

And then let's consider what the President, CEO & Chairman Willie Chiang said about the MLP's distribution policy in the Q4 2025 earnings call:

With the simplification and streamlining of our business, stable cash flow contributions from the Cactus III acquisition and reduced commodity exposure following the NGL sale, we are modestly reducing our distribution coverage ratio threshold from 160% to 150%. This reflects improved visibility for our business, better aligns us with peers, and it paves the way for future distribution growth while still maintaining a prudent level of coverage.

Already based on the non-adjusted 2026 EBITDA guide PAA had room for a slight distribution hike (i.e., going from 155% to 150% distribution coverage).

Now, with an improving result profile, it would be only reasonable to expect juicier distribution hikes.

Finally, PAA carries an investment-grade balance sheet with a well laddered debt maturity profile, where the lion's share of borrowings are based on fixed rates. In other words, the partnership is nicely positioned to reap the benefit for its top-line growth, while being largely unaffected on the cost of debt side.

Final remarks

The investment picks that I presented above are certainly solid choices to fight back inflation with current income growth. More importantly, both picks are supported by durable business models and investment grade balance sheets that come in handy in the context of potential recession.

There is a difference in yields between what BEP and PAA offer.

For BEP the yield is lower because it has a more growth-oriented strategy (e.g., huge development pipeline, active asset rotation process to free up capital for growth funding etc.), which always comes with a bit higher multiples.

PAA has lower structural growth possibilities (crude oil, which before the war with Iran was assumed to be oversupplied), but right now, due to the unexpected oil shock, it could potentially register even bigger distribution hikes than BEP (at least in the near term or until the oil prices normalize).

In a nutshell, I would suggest prudent income investors seriously consider integrating these picks into their portfolios for stress-free inflation protection.

data.title: Bitcoin: What's Really Happening Despite The Bear Market
data.description:
I think Bitcoin offers a rather favorable setup today because the price has reset far more than the adoption has. Bitcoin no longer trades like a euphoric asset, yet the buyer base keeps getting broader.

What Drives Bitcoin Today

Bitcoin still comes down to scarce new supply meeting variable but growing demand. 21 million BTC will ever exist. Less than a million are left to be mined over the next century.

Demand is no longer confined to niche exchanges and retail speculation. Traditional brokers are offering BTC and other crypto, and even including the option to withdraw to self custody.

The SEC approved spot Bitcoin ETPs on January 10, 2024, and the product set has since matured into a large pool of regulated access. IBIT’s current size on its own is enough to show that this is not a passing experiment. What’s even more remarkable is that IBIT’s options market has started to surpass Deribit in open interest and volume. Deribit is the offshore crypto options exchange that has dominated the crypto options scene for years. Combine this with the growth of CME futures on BTC and we see that the U.S. regulated derivatives complex for Bitcoin is signaling increased demand by more traditional institutions.

Corporate balance sheets are now part of that demand stack too, and this is perhaps the most underappreciated even though it is the most important. Strategy remains the main case, but it is no longer alone. Over 2025, the number of new public companies holding BTC doubled.

Almost half a million BTC—equivalent to half the remaining supply to be released over the next century—was purchased by public corporations in 2025. This does not include private companies or institutional investors.

The important thing is Bitcoin does not need every public company to become MicroStrategy. A widening corporate buyer universe is enough to drive the price much higher.

The question of course is why BTC still dropped 13% in 2025 even with so much buying. The answer is that a lot of early Bitcoin buyers were selling into this demand. Ultimately, price is set by supply and demand, and we saw an episode where the selling was stronger than the buying. What makes Bitcoin different is that there is a hard limit to how much the early BTC investors can sell, and some of the largest corporate buyers do not intend to ever sell (more on this in the next section), so the supply and demand dynamic is constantly one of moving BTC from weak hands into strong hands, which will likely lead to a supply shock.

What the Market is Missing

I think the most underappreciated near-term demand lever is Strategy’s preferred-stock machine, especially STRC. STRC is now a $5 billion asset even though it launched several months ago with a $2.5 billion IPO. Strategy issued another $2.5 billion in recent months via its ATM program, signaling the demand for this preferred stock.

But what exactly is STRC and why is it so interesting to investors?

First, I wrote a long article about it a few months ago. To keep it short, it's a preferred stock that adjusts its dividend monthly to keep the price stable at about $100 per share. There’s obviously lots of caveats to this and economic reflexivity we don’t have to get into, but the main point is that because the dividend is meant to hold the price stable, it pays a pretty high dividend. Currently 11.5%. Furthermore, due to Strategy’s negative taxable earnings and profits, the dividends are treated as return of capital, which means they go towards lowering your cost basis, effectively deferring the tax liability until you sell the stock.

Something that pays 11.5% tax-deferred while maintaining a generally stable price is clearly interesting to people. This is why they were able to double STRC’s notional value outstanding after the IPO (which was also the largest IPO in 2025). The bottom line is that when Strategy sells STRC, it takes the money to buy BTC. So from this alone, we have a good way to think about Bitcoin buy side demand:

  • How much do people want something like STRC?

  • How much of the STRC acquisition will be met by Strategy selling it rather than an existing holder selling it?

  • How much STRC can Strategy sell?

Note that I do not assume all STRC buying translates one-for-one into future Bitcoin buying by Strategy, but it is enough to say the marginal-buyer pipeline is now larger than most investors appreciate. STRC buyers are basically “partial” Bitcoin buyers that buy BTC by proxy via Strategy. And the extent of the word “partial” depends on how much STRC is being sold by Strategy vs existing holders of STRC.

Consider that in June 2025 there was no STRC. Today, $5 billion has been sold, so another $5 billion of BTC has been bought by Strategy. Without STRC, much of that $5 billion would have gone into other assets. This, I feel, is a good abstract model to understand the relationship between STRC and Bitcoin demand.

STRC is part of a broader pattern of what is increasingly being referred to as “Digital Credit”. A company will endeavor to buy BTC and then issue credit instruments against it to buy more BTC. If BTC appreciates at a faster rate than the coupon paid on the credit, then the company is creating shareholder value. The credit instrument is called “Digital Credit” because it is backed by Bitcoin, which is considered “Digital Capital.”

For Digital Credit issuers, the goal is to never sell BTC.
